record.title: Nellie Tomer Handlin, 1973-12-17, Interview - MP3 Recording
record.subject: Latah County -- Idaho
record.origin: Father's parents were among the county's first settlers, coming from California in 1871; mother was from Indiana
record.keywords: schools horses homesteads Native Americans farmers farming death gambling cemeteries families education railroads Chinese Americans stores women water winter newspapers world wars

point.title: Native Americans raced horses all over, on racetrack on grandfather's homestead
point.synopsis: Indians came from all over to bet and race horses on the race track on grandfather's homestead. Father was a friend of Mox Mox.

point.title: It took her grandparents all day to come up the old Lewiston grade, stayed overnight
point.synopsis: It took her grandparents all day to come up the old Lewiston grade, stayed overnight.

point.title: Went to Walla Walla for flour, sugar, coffee, etc., once or twice a year in the early years
point.synopsis: Went to Walla Walla for flour, sugar, coffee, etc., once or twice a year in the early years.

point.title: Site for Moscow Cemetery chosen to bury great-grandmother
point.synopsis: Site for Moscow Cemetery chosen to bury her great-grand mother. Perhaps some Indian children and grown-ups buried there previously.

point.title: Grandfather in legislature, leader of formation of Moscow school system and a farmer.
point.synopsis: Grandfather in legislature, leader of formation of Moscow school system and a farmer.

point.title: Chinese hired cheaply to build railroad. Eating out in Moscow.
point.synopsis: Chinese hired cheaply to build railroad. Eating out in Moscow.

point.title: 300 pupil Moscow High School; taking the eighth grade exam
point.synopsis: Taking the eighth grade exam. Losing her school locker when first at the 300-pupil Moscow High School. Went home on weekends.

point.title: Dob as a cashier at Penny's; open until nine o'clock Saturday night for farmers
point.synopsis: Dob as a cashier at Penny's. Had cups that went on a pulley to the office where change was made. Open until nine o'clock Saturday night, so farmers could come in.

point.title: Parents lost homestead largely as result of bad summer 1893
point.synopsis: Her folks lost their homestead largely as a result of the bad 1893 summer. They would dry out grain that hadn't matured and feed it to stock.

point.title: School; father helped with math; for eighth grade exam, had to memorize passages perfectly
point.synopsis: School. Went 1/4 mile down the road to get water for school. Father drove her two miles to school by sled in winter. Father helped her with math. For the eighth grade exam you memorized passages perfectly, including punctuation. Would help other students.

point.title: Family read to themselves after supper; WWI caused hardships on farm families who depended on sons
point.synopsis: Family read to themselves after supper. Bought books and got magazines, weekly Moscow paper and San Francisco Examiner. World War I caused hardships in farm families who depended on sons' help. No white flour during rationing in WW I.

point.title: Several Indian scares. Women and children stayed in forts and men took care of farm and stock
point.synopsis: Several Indian scares. Women and children stayed in forts and men took care of farm and stock.

record.description: Tomers' pioneering. Relations with Nez Perces. Selection of Moscow Cemetery site. School and reading as a girl. 12-17-73 1 hr LS
record.transcript: Download the PDF index; no transcript available
record.rights: Digital format copyright: University of Idaho Library. Original Copyright: Latah County Historical Society
